sections.opening: Let's begin with a precious secret from the Baal Shem Tov's tradition — every person recites daily the psalm corresponding to their age plus one. This isn't just a beautiful custom, it's a key to personal connection with the energy of your special psalm. If you're 30, you read psalm 31. If he's 45, he reads psalm 46. My grandmother used to say: "Psalms are like perfume — you need to find the fragrance that matches your soul exactly at this age." In the previous lesson we learned about the power of psalms as a source of spiritual energy, and today we'll discover how to connect to them in the most personal way — through your age and the monthly energy.
sections.theoryA:
How do I find my personal psalm according to age?

The Baal Shem Tov's tradition is simple and profound: every day you read the psalm of your age plus one. Age 30 → psalm 31, age 25 → psalm 26. Why plus one? Because we're always on our way to the next age, in a process of growth.

This isn't just a custom — it's sophisticated spiritual technology. Every age in life brings with it unique challenges, opportunities, and energies. The psalm that matches your age holds within it exactly the spiritual tools you need right now.

For example, at age 22 you read psalm 23 — "The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want." This is the age when many begin working and worrying about livelihood. This psalm teaches trust in sustenance. At age 40 you read psalm 41 — a psalm of healing. This is the age when the body begins "speaking" and you need to take better care of your health.

The Baal Shem Tov understood that every age is like a station on life's train. At each station there's a different view, different challenges, and therefore you need different tools. Your psalm is the spiritual tool custom-fitted to the point in life where you find yourself now.

This tradition also creates a connection between biological age and spiritual age. When you recite your age's psalm daily, you invite spiritual energy to enter physical life. This is a bridge between heaven and earth, between the eternal and temporal.

practice1.instruction: Calculate your age plus one and write the psalm number on paper. Read the title and message of this psalm in the table we learned. Write down how this message connects to your current life period

sections.theoryB:
How does monthly astrology connect to psalms?

The sages discovered an amazing connection between celestial zodiac signs and specific psalm chapters. Each month of the year is connected to an astrological sign, and each sign is connected to a psalm that holds the spiritual energy appropriate for this time of year. This isn't coincidence — it's a precise cosmic map.

Tishrei (Libra) connects to psalm 81 — time of new beginning and proper weighing. Cheshvan (Scorpio) connects to psalm 80 — time of depth and inner transformations. Kislev (Sagittarius) connects to psalm 30 — gratitude and Chanukah, light in winter's darkness.

Each month brings with it unique astrological energy. Libra brings a need for balance and weighing, Scorpio brings a need for depth and change, Sagittarius brings a need to expand horizons. The psalms appropriate for each month hold exactly the spiritual tools that help us work with this energy.

For example, in the month of Nissan (Aries) we read psalm 107. This is a month of new beginnings, exodus from Egypt, spring. Psalm 107 speaks of "Give thanks to the Lord for He is good" — gratitude for freedom and new beginning. In the month of Elul (Virgo) we read psalm 27 — "The Lord is my light and salvation" — a psalm of repentance and self-examination before Rosh Hashanah.

This is a dual system: you have your personal psalm by age, and you have the month's psalm by zodiac sign. These two psalms together create a perfect spiritual "blend" for what you need now — both personally and cosmically.

practice2.instruction: Check which Hebrew month we're in now. Find the appropriate psalm for this month in the list we learned. Read this psalm aloud and notice how its energy connects to your feeling about the current period

sections.deepDive:
What's not written in books — the secret of psalm 119

Psalm 119 is the longest psalm — 176 verses divided into 22 groups of 8 verses each, according to the Hebrew alphabet letters. The Chida discovered in it "a wondrous remedy beyond measure" that only few know about.

The secret is that you can read only the verses corresponding to the letters of your name. For example, if your name is "Dana" — you read the verses of the letter Dalet (25-32), the letter Nun (105-112), and the letter Heh (33-40). This creates a "personal prayer" built from psalm 119 especially for you.

Why does this work? Because the letters of your name aren't random. They were chosen by your soul before you were born, and they hold your spiritual "genetic code." When you read the verses of these letters in psalm 119, you're actually "activating" the deepest spiritual potential you have.

The Chida said this is "a wondrous remedy" because it's not just reading — it's precise spiritual technology. Every letter in the Hebrew alphabet is a creative force, and you're using the creative forces that match exactly your soul.

questions.question: According to the Baal Shem Tov's tradition, a 35-year-old reads which psalm?
questions.explanation: Age 35 + 1 = psalm 36. The tradition always says age plus one because we're on our way to the next age.

questions.question: Which psalm corresponds to the month of Tishrei (Libra)?
questions.explanation: Tishrei (Libra) corresponds to psalm 81 — a time of new beginning and proper weighing for the new year.

questions.question: How many verses are in psalm 119?
questions.explanation: Psalm 119 is the longest psalm with 176 verses divided into 22 groups of 8 verses for each letter.

questions.question: How does the Chida's remedy in psalm 119 work?
options: Read verses according to name letters
questions.explanation: The Chida discovered that reading verses corresponding to your name letters creates a personal prayer from psalm 119.

questions.question: Which month corresponds to psalm 27 (The Lord is my light and salvation)?
questions.explanation: Elul (Virgo) corresponds to psalm 27 — a time of repentance and self-examination before Rosh Hashanah.

closing.summary: Today we learned how to personally connect to psalms through two systems: the personal psalm according to your age plus one, and the monthly psalm according to astrological sign. We discovered that psalm 119 holds a special secret — the possibility to read only verses corresponding to your name letters.
closing.wisdom: The soul knows exactly which psalms it needs — you just need to listen.
closing.nextPreview: In the next lesson we'll learn about the specific remedies of each psalm and how to choose the right psalm for every life situation.

faq.question: What if I don't know which Hebrew month it is now?
faq.answer: You can find it in any Hebrew calendar or Jewish apps. The Hebrew month begins with the new moon quarter.

faq.question: Do I need to read both the age psalm and the monthly psalm every day?
faq.answer: It depends on you. The Baal Shem Tov's tradition focuses on the age psalm. The monthly psalm is a seasonal addition.

faq.question: What happens on a birthday — do I move to a new psalm?
faq.answer: Yes! On your birthday you move to the new psalm. It's like receiving a new spiritual gift for the new year.

faq.question: Does psalm 119's remedy work only with the first name or also with the last name?
faq.answer: Tradition usually refers to the first name, but some kabbalists also use the mother's name for this purpose.

crossReferences.label: 🔤 The power of letters in Kabbalah

seo.title: How to Choose Personal Psalms by Age and Zodiac - Lesson 2
seo.description: Learn the Baal Shem Tov's tradition of reading personal psalms by age, connection to monthly astrology, and the secret of psalm 119 with your name letters.
